h1Title: Acute Iridocyclitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute iridocyclitis, the most common form of uveitis, is inflammation of the iris and ciliary body inside the anterior chamber of the eye. It accounts for roughly 50-90% of all uveitis cases and carries an incidence of approximately 8-12 per 100,000 person-years in population studies. The condition typically presents with a sudden unilateral red eye, photophobia, blurred vision, and deep aching pain that worsens on accommodation. In about 50% of cases an underlying systemic disease — most often an HLA-B27-associated spondyloarthropathy — is identified. Prompt treatment with topical corticosteroids and mydriatics resolves most episodes within 4-6 weeks; delayed or inadequate treatment risks posterior synechiae, elevated intraocular pressure, cataract, and permanent vision loss.

keyStats.prevalence: Anterior uveitis incidence 8-12 per 100,000 person-years; accounts for 50-90% of all uveitis presentations (Gritz & Wong, Am J Ophthalmol 2004)
keyStats.demographics: Peak age 20-50 years; HLA-B27-positive individuals carry a 4-8% lifetime risk; men and women affected roughly equally overall, though HLA-B27-associated cases are more common in men
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in adults aged 20-50 years; pediatric cases represent about 5-10% of anterior uveitis
keyStats.globalCases: Uveitis causes 5-20% of legal blindness in developed countries; anterior uveitis is the leading uveitis subtype worldwide

definition: Acute iridocyclitis (ICD-10: H20.0) — also called anterior uveitis — is inflammation of the uveal tract limited to the iris (iritis) and ciliary body (cyclitis) within the anterior segment of the eye. When both structures are simultaneously inflamed the term iridocyclitis is preferred. The uvea is the pigmented vascular layer between the sclera and retina; its anterior portion is richly perfused and densely innervated, explaining the severity of pain in acute attacks. Inflammation breaks down the blood-aqueous barrier, releasing protein and inflammatory cells into the aqueous humor, producing the hallmark clinical signs of flare and cellular activity seen on slit-lamp examination. Episodes are classified as acute (sudden onset, duration under 3 months), recurrent (repeated episodes with intervening quiet intervals of at least 3 months off treatment), or chronic (persistent inflammation for more than 3 months). Unilateral acute non-granulomatous iridocyclitis — the most common pattern — is strongly associated with HLA-B27 (present in 50-60% of such cases) and linked to ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. Granulomatous iridocyclitis suggests sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, or herpes virus infection. Ophthalmologists manage acute episodes; rheumatologists manage underlying systemic disease.

typesClassification.description: Most common form. Sudden unilateral onset; fine keratic precipitates, aqueous flare and cells, no iris nodules. Strongly linked to HLA-B27 and spondyloarthropathies. Responds rapidly to topical steroids.

Persistent low-grade inflammation lasting more than 3 months; often bilateral and painless. Seen in juvenile idiopathic arthritis (oligoarticular type). Risk of amblyopia and band keratopathy in children.
Large mutton-fat keratic precipitates and iris nodules (Koeppe or Busacca). Causes include sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, leprosy, sympathetic ophthalmia, and Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis.
Episodic attacks separated by symptom-free intervals of at least 3 months. Typical of HLA-B27-associated disease; each episode averages 6-8 weeks. Recurrence rate is 50% within 5 years.

Caused by herpes simplex or varicella-zoster virus, Toxoplasma, syphilis, Lyme disease, or cytomegalovirus. Distinguished by clinical pattern, serology, and aqueous humor PCR. Requires antiviral or antimicrobial treatment alongside anti-inflammatory therapy.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of deep, aching eye pain that worsens when looking at bright light or focusing on near objects, often beginning overnight or on waking.
Photophobia — marked sensitivity to light — driven by painful ciliary muscle spasm triggered by light-induced pupillary constriction.
Blurred or hazy vision from aqueous flare (protein in the anterior chamber) and inflammatory cells obscuring the optical axis.
A unilateral, intensely red eye with circumcorneal (perilimbal) injection — a ring of redness around the cornea that distinguishes iridocyclitis from conjunctivitis.
Lacrimation and watery discharge without mucus or crusting, contrasting with the purulent discharge of bacterial conjunctivitis.
A small, irregular, or sluggishly reactive pupil caused by posterior synechiae (adhesions between iris and lens) or ciliary muscle spasm.
Visible keratic precipitates — white cellular deposits on the posterior corneal surface — detected on slit-lamp examination but sometimes perceptible as corneal cloudiness.
Reduced visual acuity, often 20/40 to 20/100 at presentation, partly from aqueous inflammation and partly from ciliary muscle spasm causing transient myopia.
Deep periorbital ache radiating to the temple or brow on the affected side.
Floaters or blurred halos around lights if the inflammation extends to involve the anterior vitreous.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild but progressive photophobia in one eye in a patient with known ankylosing spondylitis or HLA-B27 positivity — treated as a recurrence until proved otherwise.
Subtle circumcorneal redness without discharge, especially if associated with any eye ache, in a patient with recent back pain or joint symptoms.
A slightly smaller or less reactive pupil in the symptomatic eye noted on self-examination using a mirror in different lighting.
Brief episodes of blurred vision in one eye lasting hours that then clear, which may precede a full acute episode in patients with recurrent disease.
emergencySigns: Visual acuity below 20/200 or rapidly worsening vision over hours, suggesting severe inflammation, high intraocular pressure, or posterior segment extension.
Intraocular pressure above 30 mmHg with corneal edema (corneal haze visible to the naked eye), indicating acute secondary glaucoma that threatens the optic nerve.
Total posterior synechiae (360-degree iris-lens adhesion) causing iris bombé, anterior chamber shallowing, and acute angle-closure glaucoma — a same-day surgical emergency.
Hypopyon (visible layer of white cells in the anterior chamber) — indicates severe inflammation; must urgently exclude endophthalmitis or HLA-B27 Behcet's disease.

causes.description: HLA-B27 is present in 50-60% of patients with acute anterior uveitis and is the single strongest predictor of recurrence. The allele is found in roughly 7-8% of the general white population but in over 90% of patients with ankylosing spondylitis. Molecular mimicry between HLA-B27 and bacterial antigens from the gut microbiome is the leading pathogenic hypothesis.

causes.cause: Herpes simplex or varicella-zoster virus reactivation
causes.description: HSV-1, HSV-2, and VZV can all produce anterior uveitis through direct viral invasion or immune-mediated inflammation. Characteristic features include sectoral iris atrophy, elevated IOP disproportionate to cells, and elevated aqueous humor viral DNA by PCR. Treatment requires antiviral drugs (aciclovir or valaciclovir) in addition to corticosteroids.

Sarcoidosis causes 5-10% of anterior uveitis in tertiary referral centers. The granulomatous pattern (mutton-fat keratic precipitates, iris Busacca nodules) is characteristic. Serum ACE, chest CT, and lung biopsy guide systemic diagnosis.

Blunt or penetrating ocular trauma and intraocular surgery (cataract, vitreoretinal) trigger a breakdown of the blood-aqueous barrier and non-infectious anterior uveitis. Post-surgical inflammation usually resolves within 2-4 weeks with topical steroids.

After thorough workup, no systemic association is found in 30-50% of patients with acute anterior uveitis. These cases are managed with local anti-inflammatory therapy alone and monitored for emerging systemic disease over subsequent years.

causes.cause: Inflammatory bowel disease and reactive arthritis
causes.description: Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis together cause about 5% of anterior uveitis. Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome) is another HLA-B27-associated trigger; the classic triad of urethritis, arthritis, and uveitis develops 2-6 weeks after genitourinary or enteric infection with Chlamydia, Salmonella, Shigella, or Yersinia.

riskFactors.description: Carries a 4-8% lifetime risk of anterior uveitis; risk rises further with a coexisting spondyloarthropathy. Explains 50-60% of all acute non-granulomatous anterior uveitis in white populations.
Roughly 25-40% of AS patients develop acute anterior uveitis at some point; it is the most common extra-articular feature of AS. Risk correlates with HLA-B27 status and disease duration.
Anterior uveitis occurs in 7-25% of patients with psoriatic arthritis; tends to be bilateral and more chronic than HLA-B27 pure uveitis.
Anterior uveitis affects 4-12% of Crohn's and ulcerative colitis patients; activity often parallels gut flares, though not always.
Recurrence rate is approximately 50% within 5 years after a first episode; each subsequent attack raises the risk of complications including synechiae and glaucoma.
HIV increases risk of CMV and herpes-related anterior uveitis; immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) can trigger uveitis in patients starting antiretroviral therapy.
Outdoor occupations and high-altitude UV exposure have been associated with marginally elevated rates of anterior uveitis flares in observational cohorts, likely through phototoxic mechanisms.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-50 years account for the majority of acute cases; juvenile iridocyclitis peaks at ages 2-16 in oligoarticular JIA.
HLA-B27-associated uveitis is more prevalent in white northern European and Native American populations, where HLA-B27 allele frequency is 7-10%.
Sarcoid-associated uveitis has higher incidence in African American and Scandinavian populations.
Men and women are affected roughly equally overall, though HLA-B27-positive men have more frequent and more severe attacks.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of acute iridocyclitis is made at the slit lamp by an ophthalmologist. The essential finding is cells (white blood cells in the aqueous humor) graded on the SUN (Standardization of Uveitis Nomenclature) scale from 0.5+ (1-5 cells per high-power field) to 4+ (>50 cells). Aqueous flare, keratic precipitates, and posterior synechiae are supporting signs. The clinical history — onset, laterality, recurrence pattern, associated joint or skin disease, recent infection, or travel — directs the systemic workup. In a first episode of acute unilateral non-granulomatous uveitis in a patient aged 20-50 without systemic symptoms, many authorities recommend treatment without extensive testing, as idiopathic and HLA-B27 disease account for the great majority. Investigations are triggered by atypical features: bilateral disease, granulomatous morphology, poor steroid response, suspected infection, or systemic symptoms. A minimum workup then includes HLA-B27 typing, serum ACE and chest X-ray (sarcoid), FTA-ABS or RPR (syphilis), and QuantiFERON-TB Gold. Viral PCR of aqueous humor obtained by anterior chamber tap is reserved for suspected herpetic disease. Intraocular pressure measurement at every visit detects steroid-response glaucoma or secondary glaucoma from trabecular meshwork inflammation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents cells, flare, keratic precipitates, synechiae, and iris morphology — the definitive diagnostic examination
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Seated at a chin rest while the ophthalmologist shines a narrow beam of light into each eye through a microscope. Takes about 5-10 minutes. May require pupil dilation with drops that temporarily blur near vision for 4-6 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects elevated IOP from trabecular inflammation, corticosteroid response, or secondary angle closure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief, painless measurement done with a tonometer touching the numbed cornea or by a non-contact air-puff device. Takes under one minute.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies genetic susceptibility to spondyloarthropathy-associated uveitis; guides workup for ankylosing spondylitis and reactive arthritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Result available in 5-10 days depending on the laboratory. A positive result prompts rheumatology referral.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) and chest imaging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for sarcoidosis in granulomatous or bilateral uveitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for serum ACE; chest X-ray or CT with no radiation beyond standard screening. Together they identify hilar adenopathy or elevated ACE supporting sarcoidosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes syphilitic uveitis — a treatable mimic of almost any uveitis pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. RPR is reported within hours; FTA-ABS confirmation within 24-48 hours. All uveitis patients should be screened at least once.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects herpesvirus DNA (HSV, VZV, CMV) in atypical or steroid-unresponsive anterior uveitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief outpatient procedure under topical anaesthesia; the ophthalmologist withdraws 0.1-0.2 mL of aqueous humor with a fine needle. Mild transient discomfort. PCR result in 24-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of acute iridocyclitis pursues three goals: suppress inflammation quickly to prevent structural damage, prevent posterior synechiae by dilating the pupil, and identify and treat any underlying systemic cause. Topical corticosteroids are the cornerstone of anti-inflammatory treatment. Prednisolone acetate 1% (or difluprednate 0.05%) is started at high frequency — hourly or every 2 hours while awake — during the acute phase and tapered over 4-6 weeks according to the cellular response on slit-lamp. The American Academy of Ophthalmology (AAO) 2019 preferred practice pattern supports this approach. Mydriatics (cyclopentolate 1%, atropine 1%, or homatropine 2%) are applied twice daily to prevent synechiae and relieve ciliary spasm pain. In HLA-B27-associated disease, the combination resolves most episodes within 4-8 weeks. For moderate-to-severe or corticosteroid-dependent disease, sub-Tenon or intravitreal injection of triamcinolone acetonide provides sustained local delivery. Oral prednisone (0.5-1 mg/kg/day with taper) is reserved for severe bilateral disease, inadequate topical response, or patients unable to instill drops reliably. Systemic immunosuppression — methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, or TNF inhibitors (adalimumab) — is introduced for recurrent or chronic disease that requires more than two short courses of topical steroids per year. Adalimumab received FDA approval for non-infectious uveitis in 2016 and reduces flare frequency by approximately 50% in trials. Infectious causes require specific antimicrobial treatment alongside, not instead of, anti-inflammatory therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops (hourly in acute phase, tapered)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line topical corticosteroid for anterior uveitis. Started at 1 drop hourly while awake at peak inflammation, reduced to every 2-4 hours as cells decrease, then tapered over 4-6 weeks guided by slit-lamp response. Difluprednate 0.05% is a potent alternative requiring less-frequent dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves anterior chamber cell grade 0 in 75-85% of acute episodes within 4-6 weeks; response monitoring on slit-lamp is mandatory

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclopentolate 1% or atropine 1% eye drops (mydriatic-cycloplegic)
medicalTreatments.description: Dilates the pupil and paralyses the ciliary body, preventing synechiae formation and relieving ciliary spasm pain. Cyclopentolate 1% twice daily is standard; atropine is reserved for severe inflammation. Discontinued once active inflammation resolves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents posterior synechiae formation in over 90% of acute episodes when started promptly; analgesic effect within hours

medicalTreatments.name: Sub-Tenon triamcinolone acetonide injection (40 mg/mL)
medicalTreatments.description: Periocular corticosteroid depot placed posterior to the globe by outpatient injection under topical anaesthesia. Sustains high intraocular steroid levels for 4-8 weeks. Used in severe unilateral disease, in patients intolerant of frequent drops, or in combination with systemic therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cells and flare grade in 60-80% of treated eyes; superior to systemic oral steroids for unilateral disease in comparative studies

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisone (0.5-1 mg/kg/day, tapered over 3-6 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe bilateral disease, HLA-B27-associated attacks with posterior involvement, or failure of topical treatment. Requires bone protection, glucose monitoring, and a strict taper schedule to avoid rebound inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls acute bilateral inflammation in approximately 70-80% of cases; benefit must be weighed against systemic adverse effects of short courses

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab (Humira) 40 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: TNF-alpha inhibitor approved (FDA 2016, EMA 2016) for active non-infectious uveitis in adults, including anterior uveitis refractory to conventional immunosuppression. Requires tuberculosis and hepatitis B screening before initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces uveitis flare rate by 50% and the need for rescue oral prednisone by 60% versus placebo in the VISUAL I and II trials (PMID 27267852, 27267849)

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (7.5-25 mg weekly) or mycophenolate mofetil (1-3 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing immunosuppressants used for recurrent or chronic anterior uveitis associated with JIA, sarcoidosis, or idiopathic disease. Take 3-6 months to reach full effect; used in combination with topical treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieve corticosteroid-sparing success (able to taper prednisone to <10 mg/day) in 60-70% of patients in observational series

surgicalOptions.name: Laser peripheral iridotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Creates a small hole in the peripheral iris to relieve pupillary block and iris bombé in patients with total posterior synechiae and secondary angle closure. Outpatient laser procedure under topical anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: IOP normalizes immediately in over 90% of acute pupillary-block glaucoma cases; re-treatment needed in 10-15% over 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Synechialysis
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical or laser separation of posterior synechiae to restore pupil motility and prevent angle closure. Performed during cataract surgery or as a standalone procedure when synechiae are extensive.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful pupil dilation achieved in 70-85% of cases; recurrence risk is high without adequate ongoing anti-inflammatory treatment

surgicalOptions.name: Cataract surgery (phacoemulsification)
surgicalOptions.description: Posterior subcapsular cataract is a frequent complication of chronic iridocyclitis or prolonged corticosteroid use. Surgery is performed when inflammation has been quiescent for at least 3 months; careful perioperative steroid cover is mandatory.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual improvement in 70-85% of operated eyes; higher risk of postoperative inflammation, fibrin reaction, and visual loss compared with cataract surgery in non-uveitic eyes

alternativeTreatments.name: Dark glasses / UV-blocking sunglasses
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduces photophobic discomfort and prevents UV-mediated exacerbation; evidence-based adjunct, particularly for outdoor HLA-B27 patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm compresses
alternativeTreatments.description: Provides symptomatic relief for periorbital ache; no anti-inflammatory effect but useful patient-comfort measure during acute episodes.

whySeeSpecialist: Anterior uveitis requires slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist — the diagnosis cannot be made with the naked eye and general practitioners do not routinely have slit lamps. Systemic disease workup, second-line immunosuppression, and surgical complications also require subspecialty care. Delay beyond 24-48 hours from symptom onset risks synechiae and irreversible IOP elevation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an ophthalmologist with uveitis subspecialty training or experience in inflammatory eye disease. For recurrent or systemic-disease-associated cases, co-management with a rheumatologist (for HLA-B27 disease) or an internal medicine specialist (for sarcoidosis or IBD) optimizes outcomes. Major academic medical centers and dedicated uveitis clinics offer the broadest access to imaging and biologics.
hospitalCriteria: Slit-lamp biomicroscope and tonometer available for same-day assessment
Aqueous humor PCR capability for suspected herpetic uveitis
Access to uveitis subspecialist or comprehensive ophthalmologist with uveitis experience
Rheumatology service for systemic workup and biologic prescribing
Laser suite for iridotomy in secondary angle-closure emergencies
keyFacilities: Uveitis subspecialty clinics at academic ophthalmology departments
Laser and surgical ophthalmology day-case units
Emergency eye casualty or ocular emergency departments
preventionStrategies: Maintain regular rheumatology review and treat underlying spondyloarthropathy with disease-modifying agents; TNF inhibitors used for AS reduce uveitis flare frequency by 40-60%.
Learn to recognize prodromal photophobia early and have a supply of topical steroids available (with ophthalmologist approval) for rapid self-initiation in documented recurrent HLA-B27 uveitis.
Avoid prolonged unprotected UV exposure outdoors; wear UV-blocking wrap-around sunglasses.
Ensure up-to-date vaccination for varicella and herpes zoster (shingles vaccine) to reduce viral triggers.
Attend all scheduled ophthalmology follow-up to detect subclinical inflammation before it causes structural damage.
lifestyleModifications: Instill eye drops precisely as prescribed — frequency adherence in the first 1-2 weeks determines the risk of synechiae.
Wear wrap-around dark glasses outdoors and in bright indoor environments during acute episodes to reduce photophobic pain.
Avoid contact sports and swimming in chlorinated pools during active inflammation.
Reduce smoking — smoking is associated with more severe and more refractory uveitis in observational studies.
Maintain a uveitis flare diary recording symptom date, severity, and any identified triggers to help the ophthalmologist adjust treatment timing.
Disclose all eye symptoms promptly to rheumatologists; integrated care reduces both joint and eye disease activity.

recommended: Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) — anti-inflammatory effect supports adjunct benefit in autoimmune uveitis based on observational data
Dark green leafy vegetables rich in lutein and zeaxanthin for general ocular health
Adequate vitamin D intake — deficiency is associated with more frequent autoimmune flares in HLA-B27 disease
Antioxidant-rich foods (berries, nuts, olive oil) as general anti-inflammatory dietary support
avoid: Smoking — cigarette smoke is associated with worse outcomes in autoimmune uveitis
Excess alcohol when taking systemic immunosuppressants (methotrexate is hepatotoxic with alcohol)
High-glycaemic-index meals when on systemic corticosteroids, to limit steroid-induced glucose elevation
Excessive vitamin A supplementation, which can exacerbate inflammatory states in high doses

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise is encouraged between episodes and during mild disease on topical therapy. High-impact sports should be avoided during acute episodes when photophobia and blurred vision impair coordination and increase injury risk. Swimming in pools is contraindicated during active episodes. Once inflammation is quiescent, regular aerobic exercise supports immune regulation and reduces systemic inflammatory burden.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical corticosteroid drops at the exact scheduled frequency — missing doses in the first two weeks raises synechiae risk significantly.
Apply mydriatic drops at least 30 minutes apart from corticosteroid drops to maximize individual absorption.
Monitor vision in each eye separately every morning using a near-vision card; report any sudden worsening to the ophthalmologist the same day.
Wear photochromic or tinted lenses during daylight hours to reduce photophobic discomfort and avoid inadvertent UV exposure.
Attend slit-lamp review at 1-2 week intervals during the acute episode; do not self-taper drops ahead of schedule.
Report any increase in eye redness, worsening pain, or halos around lights — these may indicate secondary IOP elevation.
prognosis: The outlook for acute iridocyclitis depends on timeliness of treatment, recurrence pattern, and presence of underlying systemic disease. A single acute episode treated promptly with topical steroids and mydriatics resolves completely in 75-90% of patients within 4-8 weeks with no residual visual loss. Recurrent disease, occurring in approximately 50% of HLA-B27-positive patients over 5 years, carries cumulative risk: posterior synechiae develop in up to 40% of eyes with three or more recurrences, cataract in 20-30%, and chronic ocular hypertension in 10-15%. Long-term visual loss to below 20/200 occurs in roughly 5-10% of patients followed for a decade, usually from uncontrolled secondary glaucoma, cystoid macular edema, or dense posterior subcapsular cataract. Younger patients with JIA-associated chronic iridocyclitis have worse structural outcomes due to the painless nature of the disease and delayed diagnosis. Early rheumatologic referral, biologic therapy in appropriate candidates, and consistent ophthalmologic monitoring are the strongest determinants of long-term visual preservation.
recoveryTimeline: Acute pain and photophobia typically improve within 24-48 hours of starting mydriatics and frequent topical steroids. Aqueous cells reach grade 0 in most cases within 4-6 weeks. A slow steroid taper over 8-12 weeks prevents rebound inflammation. Recurrences in HLA-B27 disease last an average of 6-8 weeks each when treated promptly.
complications: Posterior synechiae — fibrous adhesions between the posterior iris and anterior lens surface — cause pupillary irregularity, impaired dilation, and, if 360-degree, iris bombé and acute glaucoma.
Secondary ocular hypertension and glaucoma from trabecular meshwork inflammation, peripheral anterior synechiae, or topical corticosteroid response; detected by tonometry at every visit.
Posterior subcapsular cataract, accelerated by both recurrent inflammation and long-term corticosteroid use; the most common long-term structural complication in recurrent disease.

Cystoid macular edema — accumulation of fluid in the central retina despite predominantly anterior inflammation — detectable by OCT; causes central visual loss that may persist months after anterior chamber quiets.
Band keratopathy — calcium deposition across the corneal surface in the interpalpebral zone — occurs in chronic iridocyclitis, particularly in JIA; treated by chelation (EDTA) or excimer laser.
Hypotony and phthisis bulbi — low IOP from ciliary body damage in severe chronic disease — leads to progressive globe shrinkage and irreversible vision loss in the most advanced cases.

supportResources.name: American Uveitis Society
supportResources.description: Professional society with patient-facing resources on uveitis types, treatment options, and finding uveitis specialists.

supportResources.name: Ocular Immunology and Uveitis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and educational resource with condition guides, a specialist directory, and clinical trial listings.

supportResources.name: AAO EyeSmart Patient Education — Uveitis
supportResources.description: American Academy of Ophthalmology patient guide covering symptoms, diagnosis, and management of uveitis.

supportResources.name: Spondylitis Association of America — Eye Disease
supportResources.description: Resources on HLA-B27 uveitis, monitoring, and coordination of care for patients with ankylosing spondylitis and related arthritis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute conjunctivitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Conjunctivitis presents with diffuse redness involving the tarsal conjunctiva, mucopurulent or watery discharge, itching, and normal vision and pupil. Iridocyclitis shows circumcorneal (perilimbal) injection, photophobia, deep aching pain, reduced acuity, and slit-lamp cells — never mucopurulent discharge. A thorough eye exam distinguishes them instantly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause acute red, painful eye with blurred vision. Angle-closure glaucoma presents with mid-dilated fixed pupil, corneal haze from edema, very high IOP (40-70 mmHg), nausea, and vomiting. Iridocyclitis shows a small constricted pupil, normal or mildly elevated IOP, and no corneal epithelial edema.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute dacryocystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dacryocystitis causes pain and swelling below the medial canthus (the tear-sac region), epiphora, and discharge — with no intraocular inflammation and normal slit-lamp findings. Iridocyclitis affects the eye interior with no external sac swelling.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic open-angle glaucoma is painless and asymptomatic until late, causing peripheral visual field loss without red eye or photophobia. Acute iridocyclitis presents with pain, redness, and photophobia at onset.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary glaucoma is a direct complication of anterior uveitis through trabecular inflammation and synechiae; both conditions require ongoing IOP monitoring.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Posterior subcapsular cataracts develop in 20-30% of eyes with recurrent iridocyclitis and are accelerated by corticosteroid therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Acquired color vision deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe or chronic iridocyclitis with cystoid macular edema can impair color discrimination as part of central visual loss.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: SLE can cause anterior and posterior uveitis; lupus patients with eye symptoms require concurrent ophthalmology and rheumatology management.

faqs.question: What is acute iridocyclitis?
faqs.answer: Acute iridocyclitis is inflammation of the iris and ciliary body inside the eye. It produces a red, painful, light-sensitive eye with blurred vision and is the most common form of uveitis. Most cases are linked to an immune condition (commonly HLA-B27-associated arthritis) or are idiopathic. It is treated with topical steroid and dilating eye drops.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of iridocyclitis?
faqs.answer: The classic symptoms are sudden deep eye pain, extreme light sensitivity (photophobia), blurred vision, and a red ring around the cornea (circumcorneal injection). The pupil may appear small or irregular. There is no sticky discharge, which helps distinguish it from conjunctivitis.

faqs.question: What causes acute iridocyclitis?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is HLA-B27-associated spondyloarthropathy (ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis). Herpesvirus reactivation, sarcoidosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and ocular trauma are other causes. In 30-50% of cases no underlying cause is found after workup.

faqs.question: How is iridocyclitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist, which shows cells and flare in the anterior chamber. A full workup includes HLA-B27 typing, serum ACE, syphilis serology, and chest imaging. PCR of aqueous fluid is performed for suspected viral disease.

faqs.question: What is the first-line treatment for acute iridocyclitis?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops given hourly in the acute phase, tapered over 4-6 weeks. A mydriatic drop (cyclopentolate 1%) is added twice daily to dilate the pupil, prevent adhesions, and relieve pain.

faqs.question: How long does an episode of iridocyclitis last?
faqs.answer: With prompt treatment, most acute episodes resolve in 4-8 weeks. In patients with HLA-B27-associated disease, each episode averages 6-8 weeks. Without treatment, episodes can persist for months and cause permanent structural damage.

faqs.question: Can iridocyclitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence occurs in approximately 50% of patients within 5 years, especially in HLA-B27-positive individuals. Each recurrence raises the risk of complications including synechiae, glaucoma, and cataract. Biologic drugs (adalimumab) reduce the flare frequency in recurrent disease.

faqs.question: Can iridocyclitis lead to blindness?
faqs.answer: Permanent vision loss below 20/200 occurs in roughly 5-10% of patients with recurrent disease over 10 years, usually from secondary glaucoma, dense posterior subcapsular cataract, or cystoid macular edema. Early treatment and consistent monitoring substantially reduce this risk.

faqs.question: Is iridocyclitis the same as uveitis?
faqs.answer: Iridocyclitis is the most common subtype of uveitis. Uveitis refers to inflammation anywhere in the uveal tract (iris, ciliary body, choroid). Iridocyclitis and anterior uveitis are synonymous terms for inflammation limited to the iris and ciliary body, distinguished from intermediate uveitis (pars plana) and posterior uveitis (choroid or retina).

faqs.question: What is posterior synechiae and why is it dangerous?
faqs.answer: Posterior synechiae are fibrous adhesions that form between the back of the iris and the front surface of the lens when inflammation is not adequately controlled. They prevent the pupil from dilating, can cause iris bombé and acute glaucoma, and increase cataract risk. Prompt treatment with mydriatic drops prevents their formation.

faqs.question: Does ankylosing spondylitis always cause eye problems?
faqs.answer: About 25-40% of patients with ankylosing spondylitis develop at least one episode of acute anterior uveitis during their lifetime. It is the most common extra-articular feature of AS. HLA-B27-positive AS patients have the highest risk. Uveitis can precede AS joint symptoms by years in some patients.

faqs.question: Is adalimumab used for uveitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adalimumab (Humira) received FDA approval in 2016 for active non-infectious uveitis in adults. It reduces uveitis flare frequency by approximately 50% and the need for systemic steroids by 60% compared with placebo in VISUAL I and II trials. It is used for recurrent or chronic disease not controlled with conventional immunosuppression.

faqs.question: Should I go to the emergency department for a sudden red painful eye?
faqs.answer: Yes. A sudden red, painful eye with photophobia and blurred vision should be assessed the same day — either by an ophthalmologist or in an eye emergency department. Conditions requiring urgent treatment include iridocyclitis, acute angle-closure glaucoma, and endophthalmitis. Do not wait to see if it resolves on its own.

faqs.question: Can iridocyclitis affect both eyes?
faqs.answer: Acute HLA-B27-associated iridocyclitis is almost always unilateral, affecting one eye at a time, though attacks may alternate between eyes over years. Chronic iridocyclitis from JIA or sarcoidosis is more often bilateral. Simultaneous bilateral acute iridocyclitis should prompt evaluation for syphilis or Behcet's disease.

faqs.question: What happens if iridocyclitis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated iridocyclitis leads to posterior synechiae, secondary glaucoma from pupillary block or trabecular damage, posterior subcapsular cataract, and cystoid macular edema. Prolonged uncontrolled inflammation can cause permanent vision loss and, in severe cases, phthisis bulbi (shrinkage and blindness of the eye).

metaDescription: Acute iridocyclitis (anterior uveitis): eye pain, photophobia and blurred vision from iris inflammation. Causes, steroid treatment and prognosis reviewed.
